5003 Homework 3¶

Author： LAN Tianwei 藍天蔚
ID： 21230969
Email： tlanaa@connect.ust.hk

(a) Calculate the Autocorrelation function of $s_0$¶

In [ ]:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def s_0(x):     # s_0(x) = sin(2πx) · I(-3 ≤ x ≤ 3)
    return np.where((x >= -3) & (x <= 3), np.sin(2 * np.pi * x), 0.0)  

# Generate signal
N = 2000
x = np.linspace(-6, 6, N)
dx = x[1] - x[0]            # step length
s_0 = s_0(x)

# Use discrete rectangle area to calculate approximate integral 
lags = np.arange(-N+1, N)
raw  = np.correlate(s_0, s_0, mode='full') # the correlate function is very useful here
R_discrete = raw * dx
tau = lags * dx

# normalization
R0 = R_discrete[N-1]
R_discrete /= R0

plt.figure(figsize=(6,3))
plt.plot(tau, R_discrete)
plt.title('Autocorrelation of $s_0(x)$ (pure NumPy)')
plt.xlabel(r'$\tau$'); plt.ylabel(r'$R(\tau)/R(0)$'); plt.grid()
plt.show()

(b) Calculate the convolution $(s*r)(x)$¶

In [31]:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def s_0(x):     # s_0(x) = sin(2πx) · I(-3 ≤ x ≤ 3)
    return np.where((x >= -3) & (x <= 3), np.sin(2 * np.pi * x), 0.0)  

def p(x):     # p_0(x) = sin(20πx) · I(-3 ≤ x ≤ 3)
    return np.where((x >= -3) & (x <= 3), np.sin(20 * np.pi * x), 0.0)  

# Generate signals
N = 2000
x = np.linspace(-6, 6, N)
p = p(x)
s = s_0(x) + p
dx = x[1] - x[0]

# Define Gaussian response function
def make_gaussian_kernel(sigma, dx):
    kernel_half_width = int(3 * sigma / dx) + 1
    t = np.arange(-kernel_half_width, kernel_half_width + 1) * dx
    kernel = np.exp(-t**2 / (2 * sigma**2))
    kernel /= np.sum(kernel)  # normalize so sum(kernel) = 1 (discrete probability)
    return kernel

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
plt.plot(x, s, label='Original Signal s(x)', color='gray', linewidth=1.5, alpha=0.8)


kernel = make_gaussian_kernel(0.2, dx)
conv_result = np.convolve(s, kernel, mode='same')  # NO * dx here!
plt.plot(x, conv_result, label=f'Convolved (σ = {0.2})', linewidth=2)

# Plot settings
plt.xlim(-4, 4)
plt.xlabel('x')
plt.ylabel('Amplitude')
plt.title('Convolution with Properly Constructed Gaussian Kernels')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('convolution_fixed.png', dpi=150)
plt.show()

Bonus: Try different sigmas¶

In [29]:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def s_0(x):     # s_0(x) = sin(2πx) · I(-3 ≤ x ≤ 3)
    return np.where((x >= -3) & (x <= 3), np.sin(2 * np.pi * x), 0.0)  

def p(x):     # p_0(x) = sin(20πx) · I(-3 ≤ x ≤ 3)
    return np.where((x >= -3) & (x <= 3), np.sin(20 * np.pi * x), 0.0)  

# Generate signals
N = 2000
x = np.linspace(-6, 6, N)
p = p(x)
s = s_0(x) + p
dx = x[1] - x[0]

# Define Gaussian response function
def make_gaussian_kernel(sigma, dx):
    kernel_half_width = int(3 * sigma / dx) + 1
    t = np.arange(-kernel_half_width, kernel_half_width + 1) * dx
    kernel = np.exp(-t**2 / (2 * sigma**2))
    kernel /= np.sum(kernel)  # normalize so sum(kernel) = 1 (discrete probability)
    return kernel

sigmas = [0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0]

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
plt.plot(x, s, label='Original Signal s(x)', color='lightgray', linewidth=1.5, alpha=0.8)

for sigma in sigmas:
    kernel = make_gaussian_kernel(sigma, dx)
    conv_result = np.convolve(s, kernel, mode='same')  # NO * dx here!
    plt.plot(x, conv_result, label=f'Convolved (σ = {sigma})', linewidth=2)

# Plot settings
plt.xlim(-4, 4)
plt.xlabel('x')
plt.ylabel('Amplitude')
plt.title('Convolution with Properly Constructed Gaussian Kernels')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('convolution_fixed.png', dpi=150)
plt.show()
